events.text: The patient is a 47-year-old female named Margaret Osei-Bonsu. She is of West African (Ghanaian) descent, born in Accra and emigrated to the United States at age 22. She is a nonsmoker with no significant alcohol use. She works as a secondary school mathematics teacher. She has two children. She has a family history notable for her maternal aunt diagnosed with breast cancer at age 38 and her maternal grandmother with ovarian cancer at age 55, prompting prior genetic counseling referral.

At age 47 years, the patient received a diagnosis of high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) of the bilateral ovaries and fallopian tubes, FIGO stage IIIC (AJCC 8th edition pT3c pN1 M0). The primary site is coded as ovary (ICD-O-3 C56.9), with synchronous fallopian tube involvement (C57.0). Histology code: 8461/3 (serous cystadenocarcinoma, NOS). The tumor is high-grade (FIGO grade 3). Summary stage: regional/distant (peritoneal). Relevant staging data: ascites cytology positive for malignant cells; peritoneal carcinomatosis involving the omentum, right and left pelvic peritoneum, and multiple small bowel mesenteric implants measuring up to 2.3 cm; retroperitoneal lymph node involvement (bilateral pelvic and para-aortic nodes, largest 1.8 cm). No parenchymal liver or lung metastases. CA-125 at diagnosis: 3,847 U/mL. HE4: 412 pmol/L. Confirmation: positive histology via omentectomy specimen. Tumor size: bilateral ovarian masses, right 7.2 cm, left 5.8 cm.

At age 47 years, the patient had an anatomic pathology report from diagnostic laparoscopy and omental biopsy. Gross: omental cake, 14 x 9 x 3 cm, gray-white firm tissue. Microscopic: sheets and papillary clusters of highly atypical epithelial cells with prominent nucleoli, marked nuclear pleomorphism, and abundant mitotic figures including atypical forms. Psammoma bodies present. Stromal desmoplasia noted. Immunohistochemistry: WT-1 positive (diffuse), PAX8 positive (diffuse), p53 aberrant (diffuse strong overexpression pattern, consistent with TP53 gain-of-function mutation), ER weakly positive (30%), CK7 positive, CK20 negative, CA-125 positive. Ki-67 proliferation index: 78%. Morphologic and IHC profile consistent with high-grade serous carcinoma of Mullerian origin. No mucinous, endometrioid, or clear cell features identified. Peritoneal washing cytology: positive for malignant epithelial cells with serous features.

At age 47 years, the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV and oral contrast (pre-operative staging). Chest: no pleural effusion, no pulmonary nodules, no mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Abdomen and pelvis: large complex right adnexal mass measuring 7.2 x 6.1 x 5.8 cm with solid and cystic components and internal septations; left adnexal mass 5.8 x 4.3 cm with similar features; moderate volume ascites; omental caking with confluent soft tissue density replacing the omentum measuring up to 11 cm in maximum dimension; multiple peritoneal implants along the right paracolic gutter, left pelvic peritoneum, and small bowel mesentery (largest 2.3 cm); right external iliac lymph node 1.6 cm, left para-aortic lymph node 1.8 cm. Liver: no parenchymal metastases, mild perihepatic ascites. Spleen: normal. No hydronephrosis. Impression: extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis with bilateral adnexal masses, omental caking, and lymphadenopathy consistent with advanced ovarian primary. Disease appears surgically resectable; no suprarenal or supradiaphragmatic disease. Peritoneal Cancer Index (PCI) estimated at 18.

At age 47 years, the patient had germline genetic testing and tumor next-generation sequencing performed. Germline testing (blood): BRCA1 pathogenic variant c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756ProfsTer74), also known as 5382insC, classified as pathogenic (ClinVar ID 37523). This is a founder mutation prevalent in populations of Eastern European Ashkenazi Jewish and also independently reported in West African populations. Secondary findings: no BRCA2 pathogenic variant identified; no Lynch syndrome gene mutations (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) detected. Somatic tumor NGS (Foundation One CDx on omental biopsy specimen): TP53 p.R248W (VAF 68%, pathogenic, gain-of-function), BRCA1 p.Gln1756ProfsTer74 (VAF 82%, consistent with loss of heterozygosity at BRCA1 locus), RB1 deep deletion (homozygous loss), CCNE1 amplification (copy number 6.2), NF1 p.R1362* (VAF 18%, subclonal, likely pathogenic), PIK3CA p.E545K (VAF 12%, subclonal), NOTCH3 p.R1626C (VAF 9%, variant of uncertain significance). Tumor mutational burden: 4.2 mut/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: microsatellite stable (MSS). Genomic loss of heterozygosity (gLOH): 31% (high). HRD score: 68 (positive). BRCA1 promoter methylation: absent. No NTRK fusions detected. No AR amplification. No ERBB2 amplification. No CDK12 mutation. Actionable alterations: germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant with somatic LOH (highly predictive of PARP inhibitor sensitivity), high HRD score.

At age 47 years, the patient had a gynecologic oncology pre-operative consultation note. The patient presented with a 4-month history of progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, and a 6 kg unintentional weight loss. She reported bilateral lower pelvic pressure and urinary frequency. On review of systems, she noted fatigue but denied nausea, vomiting, or change in bowel habits. Performance status: ECOG 1. Physical examination: abdomen distended with a fluid wave consistent with moderate ascites; a firm, fixed pelvic mass palpable extending to the umbilicus; no supraclavicular or inguinal lymphadenopathy. Assessment: advanced high-grade serous ovarian cancer, FIGO IIIC, with peritoneal carcinomatosis and ascites. Surgical complexity score estimated as moderate-high. Based on imaging and diagnostic laparoscopy findings, patient is deemed a candidate for primary debulking surgery (PDS) with the goal of achieving optimal cytoreduction (R0). Patient counseled extensively on surgery, risks, chemotherapy, and genetic implications for family members. Patient is emotionally distressed and requires additional psychosocial support; referral placed to oncology social work and gynecologic oncology support group.

At age 47 years, the patient underwent primary debulking surgery (PDS). Procedure: exploratory laparotomy, total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (TAH-BSO), infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, resection of peritoneal implants from the right paracolic gutter, sigmoid mesentery, and left pelvic sidewall, and diaphragmatic stripping of a 1.5 cm right hemidiaphragm implant. No bowel resection required. Estimated blood loss: 950 mL; 2 units packed red blood cells transfused intraoperatively. Operative time: 5 hours 40 minutes. Surgical findings: bilateral ovarian masses as described on imaging, moderate volume ascites (2.8 L drained and sent for cytology), omental cake, and peritoneal implants as described. At conclusion of surgery: no gross residual disease (R0 resection achieved). Surgeon's assessment: complete gross resection. Postoperative course: patient recovered in the surgical ICU overnight for monitoring, transferred to floor on postoperative day 1. No major postoperative complications. Discharged on postoperative day 5 with VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily for 28 days).

At age 47 years, the patient had a definitive surgical pathology report from primary debulking surgery. Right ovary (7.2 cm): high-grade serous carcinoma extensively involving the ovarian parenchyma and surface, with vascular invasion identified. Left ovary (5.8 cm): high-grade serous carcinoma with surface involvement. Right fallopian tube: serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) of the fimbriated end with invasive carcinoma involving the tubal wall, supporting fallopian tube origin of primary tumor. Left fallopian tube: STIC without invasive carcinoma. Uterus: no myometrial invasion; endometrium atrophic. Omentum: extensive replacement by metastatic high-grade serous carcinoma (omental cake). Appendix: one peritoneal implant on appendiceal serosa. Right paracolic gutter peritoneum: 3 implants, largest 2.1 cm. Left pelvic sidewall peritoneum: 2 implants, largest 1.4 cm. Right hemidiaphragm peritoneum: 1 implant, 1.5 cm. Pelvic lymph nodes: 4 of 18 positive for metastatic carcinoma (right external iliac x2, left obturator x1, right obturator x1). Para-aortic lymph nodes: 1 of 9 positive (right para-aortic). Ascites cytology: positive for malignant cells. Pathologic stage: pT3c pN1 (AJCC 8th). Margins: all surgical margins negative. No residual macroscopic disease.

At age 47 years, approximately 6 weeks post-surgery, the patient had a post-operative CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast (pre-chemotherapy baseline). Post-surgical changes in the pelvis including expected fluid and soft tissue stranding at the operative bed. No evidence of residual pelvic or abdominal masses. No ascites. Post-surgical lymphocele measuring 2.1 cm in the right obturator fossa, no intervention needed. No new peritoneal implants identified. No pleural effusion. No pulmonary nodules. Impression: expected post-operative appearance following debulking surgery with no evidence of residual macroscopic disease. Appropriate baseline for monitoring response to chemotherapy.

At age 47 years, the patient had a medical oncology new patient consultation prior to initiating adjuvant chemotherapy. CA-125 at 6 weeks post-surgery: 187 U/mL (down from 3,847 U/mL at diagnosis, consistent with successful cytoreduction but not yet normalized). ECOG performance status: 1 (recovering well from surgery, mild fatigue and incisional discomfort). Labs: CBC within normal limits (WBC 6.8, Hgb 11.4, platelets 289); comprehensive metabolic panel normal; creatinine 0.82 mg/dL; eGFR >60. Discussed treatment plan: adjuvant carboplatin AUC 5/paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days for 6 cycles, followed by PARP inhibitor maintenance given germline BRCA1 status. Patient educated on chemotherapy side effects including neuropathy, alopecia, myelosuppression, and hypersensitivity reactions. Patient expressed significant anxiety about hair loss; referral to oncology wig program. Discussed PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy for up to 3 years. Treatment plan concordant with GOG-0172 / ICON7 evidence base.

At age 47 years, the patient initiated adjuvant chemotherapy with carboplatin (AUC 5, calculated dose 525 mg IV) and paclitaxel (175 mg/m2, 305 mg IV) administered intravenously every 21 days. Cycle 1 administered without incident. Standard premedications given: dexamethasone 20 mg IV, diphenhydramine 25 mg IV, famotidine 20 mg IV. Paclitaxel infused over 3 hours without hypersensitivity reaction. Carboplatin infused over 30 minutes without reaction. Patient tolerated cycle 1 well with mild nausea managed with ondansetron and prochlorperazine. No hypersensitivity reaction. Growth factor support (pegfilgrastim) administered 24 hours post-chemotherapy per protocol.

At age 47 years, during cycle 2 of carboplatin/paclitaxel chemotherapy, the patient experienced a Grade 2 hypersensitivity reaction to carboplatin during the infusion (facial flushing, urticaria, mild chest tightness). Infusion was stopped, treated with diphenhydramine 50 mg IV and hydrocortisone 100 mg IV with resolution of symptoms within 20 minutes. Subsequently, carboplatin was re-challenged with a slow titration desensitization protocol using a 12-step infusion protocol. Patient completed the dose without further reaction. For subsequent cycles, the patient received extended premedication and slow infusion per allergy/desensitization protocol. Additionally, the patient reported Grade 2 alopecia (complete scalp hair loss) and Grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy in the fingertips bilaterally.

At age 47 years, mid-treatment assessment after cycle 3 of carboplatin/paclitaxel. CA-125: 42 U/mL (declining from 187 pre-treatment; approaching normal range). Patient reports fatigue (Grade 2), neuropathy fingertips and toes (Grade 1-2), and complete alopecia. No febrile neutropenia episodes. CBC: WBC 4.1, ANC 2.2, Hgb 10.8 (Grade 1 anemia), platelets 198. ECOG performance status: 1. Patient managing side effects with dose modifications discussed but not yet needed. Neuropathy monitoring closely. Electrolytes repleted as needed. Nausea well-controlled with prophylactic antiemetics. Patient participating in oncology support group and reports improved coping. Restaging imaging deferred to post-cycle 6 per standard protocol.

At age 47 years, following cycle 4 of carboplatin/paclitaxel, the patient was seen in urgent care for Grade 3 fatigue and Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy (sensory, bilateral hands and feet, affecting instrumental activities of daily living). CBC showed Grade 2 anemia (Hgb 8.9 g/dL). Patient received a transfusion of 2 units packed red blood cells with improvement in Hgb to 10.6 g/dL. Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents not initiated given ongoing curative-intent therapy. Paclitaxel dose reduced to 135 mg/m2 (reduction of approximately 20%) for cycles 5 and 6 given cumulative neuropathy to prevent further progression. Patient counseled on the neuropathy management with duloxetine 30 mg daily started.

At age 47 years, the patient completed cycles 5 and 6 of carboplatin (AUC 5)/paclitaxel (dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2) per modified schedule following paclitaxel dose reduction. Carboplatin continued at full dose with desensitization protocol for hypersensitivity. Both cycles completed without new hypersensitivity reactions or dose-limiting toxicities beyond previously noted neuropathy. Cycle 6 completed, marking the end of primary adjuvant chemotherapy. Total cumulative paclitaxel dose: approximately 1,580 mg/m2 (accounting for dose reduction). Patient tolerated remaining cycles reasonably well.

At age 47 years, approximately 4 weeks after completion of cycle 6 chemotherapy, the patient had a post-treatment restaging CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast. No evidence of disease recurrence or residual disease. The previously noted right obturator lymphocele has decreased to 0.8 cm. No new lymphadenopathy. No ascites. No pleural effusion. No pulmonary nodules or masses. Post-surgical changes in the pelvis are stable. No peritoneal nodules or omental mass identified. Impression: No evidence of disease (NED). Complete radiographic response following primary debulking surgery and adjuvant chemotherapy. Findings concordant with biochemical response.

At age 47 years, the patient had a post-chemotherapy assessment prior to initiating maintenance therapy. CA-125: 11 U/mL (normalized; within normal range <35 U/mL). HE4: 48 pmol/L (normalized). ECOG performance status: 1. Peripheral neuropathy: improved to Grade 1 (mild tingling in toes bilaterally, no functional limitation). Hair beginning to regrow. CBC: WBC 6.4, ANC 3.9, Hgb 11.8, platelets 241. Liver function tests and metabolic panel: normal. Creatinine: 0.79 mg/dL. Comprehensive response to therapy: complete biochemical and radiographic response (NED). Patient eligible for PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy per PRIMA/SOLO-1 trial evidence given germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant and complete response to platinum-based chemotherapy. Discussed options: niraparib (Zejula) vs olaparib (Lynparza) maintenance; given single-agent dosing convenience and FDA approval, niraparib selected. Patient counseled on niraparib side effects: nausea, fatigue, anemia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia, hypertension. Baseline weight: 64 kg, baseline platelets 241,000/uL; weight-based dosing selected: niraparib 200 mg daily (weight <77 kg and baseline platelets <150,000/uL criterion not met, but per PRIMA individualized dosing: weight <77 kg → start at 200 mg per updated prescribing guidance). Plan for CBC monitoring every week for first 2 months, then monthly.

At age 47 years (approximately 8 months after initial diagnosis), the patient initiated niraparib (PARP inhibitor) maintenance therapy at a starting dose of 200 mg orally once daily, taken at bedtime to reduce nausea. Concurrent antiemetic: ondansetron 8 mg as needed and prochlorperazine PRN. Antihypertensive monitoring initiated. Concurrent medications: duloxetine 30 mg daily (for neuropathy), ondansetron PRN, multivitamin. Patient instructed on CBC monitoring schedule: weekly CBCs for the first 8 weeks.

At age 47 years, approximately 3 weeks after starting niraparib maintenance, the patient presented for scheduled CBC monitoring. Results: WBC 4.8, Hgb 11.2, platelets 72,000/uL (Grade 3 thrombocytopenia, CTCAE v5.0). Patient denied active bleeding, petechiae, epistaxis, or bruising. Physical examination: no petechiae or purpura noted; no evidence of bleeding. Niraparib held per protocol. Repeat CBC in 1 week: platelets 58,000/uL (persisting Grade 3 thrombocytopenia). Hematology consulted: peripheral blood smear showed reduced platelet numbers with normal platelet morphology, no schistocytes or microangiopathic changes; DIC panel negative; no evidence of immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP); consistent with drug-induced thrombocytopenia from PARP inhibitor. Niraparib continued to be held. CBC checked again at week 5: platelets recovered to 118,000/uL. Niraparib restarted at dose-reduced level of 100 mg orally once daily.

At age 47 years, the patient had an oncology follow-up visit 8 weeks after niraparib dose reduction to 100 mg daily. CBC: WBC 5.1, Hgb 11.4, platelets 142,000/uL (platelets recovering, now Grade 1 thrombocytopenia). No bleeding episodes since dose reduction. Patient reports mild nausea (Grade 1, managed with PRN ondansetron), fatigue (Grade 1-2), and mild headaches (Grade 1). Blood pressure: 138/86 mmHg (elevated); niraparib can cause hypertension; amlodipine 5 mg daily initiated in coordination with primary care physician. ECOG performance status: 1. CA-125: 9 U/mL (remains within normal range). Patient tolerating reduced dose of niraparib 100 mg daily without further Grade 3 thrombocytopenia. Plan: continue niraparib 100 mg daily; CBC monthly; CA-125 every 3 months; CT restaging at 12 months from completion of chemotherapy.

At age 47 years, at the 3-month follow-up on niraparib 100 mg daily maintenance, CBC revealed mild thrombocytopenia recurrence: platelets 98,000/uL (Grade 2). Patient asymptomatic, no bleeding. Niraparib continued without further dose reduction given Grade 2 event at already-reduced dose of 100 mg; per prescribing information, niraparib may be continued with Grade 2 thrombocytopenia with close monitoring. Patient counseled to avoid NSAIDs, aspirin, and activities with high bleeding risk. Additional blood pressure monitoring: 142/88 mmHg; amlodipine dose increased to 10 mg daily. Repeat CBC in 2 weeks: platelets 109,000/uL (stable Grade 2). Continued monitoring monthly.

At age 48 years (12 months after completion of adjuvant chemotherapy, approximately 20 months after initial diagnosis), the patient had a surveillance CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast. Chest: no pulmonary nodules, no pleural effusion, no mediastinal adenopathy. Abdomen and pelvis: no evidence of peritoneal implants, no ascites, no omental mass, no lymphadenopathy. Post-surgical changes in the pelvis are stable and unremarkable. No new findings. Impression: No evidence of disease recurrence. Complete and sustained radiographic remission maintained on niraparib maintenance therapy.

At age 48 years, the patient had a routine oncology follow-up at 12 months on niraparib maintenance therapy (20 months from initial diagnosis). CA-125: 8 U/mL (sustained normal). Platelets stable at 105,000/uL (Grade 1-2 thrombocytopenia, chronic and manageable on 100 mg dosing). Blood pressure: 132/82 mmHg (improved on amlodipine 10 mg). Neuropathy: Grade 1, stable, tolerable. ECOG performance status: 0-1. Patient has returned to full-time teaching. Patient describes improved emotional wellbeing and engagement with support group. Discussed ongoing PARP inhibitor maintenance for total duration of 3 years per SOLO-1 trial evidence in germline BRCA1 patients achieving complete response. Patient encouraged regarding excellent prognosis with R0 resection, complete biochemical and radiographic response, germline BRCA1 status, and no evidence of recurrence at 20 months. Discussed family member genetic testing: patient's daughter (age 22) and sister (age 44) referred for genetic counseling.

At age 48 years (24 months from initial diagnosis, 16 months on niraparib maintenance), the patient had a scheduled oncology assessment. CA-125: 7 U/mL (persistently normal). CBC: WBC 5.3, Hgb 12.1, platelets 114,000/uL (Grade 1 thrombocytopenia, stable, chronic on niraparib 100 mg). Patient reports no new symptoms. Physical examination: no palpable masses, no ascites, no lymphadenopathy. Blood pressure: 128/80 mmHg (well-controlled). Quality of life: patient reports good functional status, returned to all normal activities including hiking and yoga. Persistent mild peripheral neuropathy (Grade 1) in bilateral feet, stable, not limiting. No evidence of disease by examination or labs. Next imaging: CT at 24 months from chemotherapy completion (approximately 4 months away). Patient continues niraparib 100 mg daily with excellent tolerance at this dose. Total planned duration of niraparib: 3 years from initiation.

At age 48 years (24 months after completion of adjuvant chemotherapy, 28 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a surveillance CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast. Chest: no nodules, no effusions. Abdomen: liver, spleen, kidneys, pancreas unremarkable. No ascites. Pelvis: post-hysterectomy/BSO changes, no masses or nodules. No lymphadenopathy above or below the diaphragm. No peritoneal thickening or implants identified. Impression: No evidence of disease recurrence. Ongoing complete remission.

At age 48-49 years (30 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had an interval oncology follow-up. CA-125: 10 U/mL (normal). The patient reported a new symptom of mild right flank discomfort for 2 weeks. Urinalysis obtained: trace hematuria noted. CT urogram obtained (see imaging report). CBC: WBC 5.0, Hgb 11.9, platelets 118,000/uL (stable). Niraparib continued. Urologic consult placed. Flank pain attributed to a 4 mm non-obstructing right renal calculus on CT urogram; no evidence of cancer recurrence. Managed conservatively with hydration and tamsulosin; stone passed spontaneously within 3 weeks.

At age 49 years (30 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a CT urogram performed to evaluate right flank pain and hematuria. Kidneys: 4 mm non-obstructing calculus in the right lower pole calyx without hydronephrosis or hydroureter. No upper tract urothelial lesions. Bilateral kidneys otherwise normal in size and enhancement. Bladder: normal. Pelvis: post-operative changes from prior hysterectomy/BSO, no pelvic masses, no ascites, no peritoneal implants. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Impression: 4 mm non-obstructing right renal calculus. No evidence of malignancy or disease recurrence. Findings unrelated to ovarian cancer.

At age 49 years (36 months from initial diagnosis, 28 months on niraparib maintenance), the patient had a scheduled oncology visit marking the planned end of niraparib maintenance therapy. CA-125: 9 U/mL (persistently normal). CBC: WBC 5.4, Hgb 12.3, platelets 128,000/uL (recovered to near-normal off extended treatment; platelets trending up over last 3 checks). Patient has completed 28 months of niraparib 100 mg daily (total intended 36 months; treatment duration slightly shortened due to cumulative tolerability and shared decision-making discussion regarding risks/benefits at this time point per updated SOLO-1 and PRIMA data). ECOG PS: 0. Patient feels well, no symptoms of concern. Peripheral neuropathy: largely resolved (Grade 0-1, mild residual tingling in toes only). Blood pressure: 122/76 mmHg (normotensive, amlodipine being tapered). Discussed cessation of niraparib: niraparib discontinued. Plan: transition to surveillance with CA-125 every 3 months and CT every 6 months for next 2 years, then annually thereafter. Patient counseled that she has an excellent prognosis given R0 resection, germline BRCA1 status with complete response, and sustained remission at 36 months. Discussed annual breast MRI and mammography given germline BRCA1 status. Risk-reducing mastectomy discussed; patient declines at this time but agrees to intensive surveillance.

At age 49 years (40 months from initial diagnosis, 4 months post-niraparib cessation), the patient had a surveillance follow-up. CA-125: 11 U/mL (normal). No new symptoms. ECOG PS: 0. Physical examination: unremarkable. Blood pressure: 118/74 mmHg (normotensive, amlodipine successfully tapered off). CBC: WBC 5.9, Hgb 12.8, platelets 198,000/uL (platelets fully recovered to normal range following niraparib discontinuation). Next surveillance CT scheduled in 2 months (at 42 months from diagnosis). Breast surveillance: recent bilateral breast MRI at an outside institution showed no suspicious findings; report obtained and filed. Patient continues to do well and is in sustained complete remission. Neuropathy fully resolved. Patient engaged in regular physical activity and yoga. Discussed continued genetic implications: patient's daughter proceeded with genetic testing and was found to be a BRCA1 carrier; referred for risk-reduction counseling.

documents.text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Mid-Treatment Assessment — Cycle 3 of 6 Paraplatin/PTX

ATTENDING: [Attending Gynecologic Medical Oncologist]
DEPARTMENT: Medical Oncology / Gynecologic Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established Patient Follow-Up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 47-year-old Ghanaian-American woman with high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC, presenting today for mid-treatment assessment following completion of cycle 3 of adjuvant carbo (AUC 5) / PTX (175 mg/m2, now reduced as discussed below) chemotherapy. She presents for toxicity review, laboratory evaluation, CA-125 surveillance, and treatment planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 47-year-old woman with germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC, 5382insC founder mutation) who was diagnosed with high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0) approximately four months ago following presentation with progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, and a 6 kg unintentional weight loss. Her CA-125 at diagnosis was markedly elevated at 3,847 U/mL. She underwent primary debulking surgery (TAH-BSO, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, peritoneal implant resection, and right hemidiaphragm stripping), achieving complete gross resection (R0). Final pathology confirmed pT3c pN1 HGSC with 4 of 18 pelvic lymph nodes and 1 of 9 para-aortic nodes positive, and serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) of the right fimbriated end. Post-operative CA-125 at 6 weeks was 187 U/mL, still declining but not yet normalized. A pre-chemotherapy CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed no residual macroscopic disease.

She initiated adjuvant chemotherapy with carbo AUC 5 / PTX 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days. Cycle 1 was tolerated without incident. During cycle 2, she experienced a Grade 2 carbo hypersensitivity reaction (facial flushing, urticaria, and mild chest tightness), which was managed by stopping the infusion, administering diphenhydramine 50 mg IV and hydrocortisone 100 mg IV, and successfully completing the dose using a 12-step desensitization protocol. Subsequent cycles have been administered with extended premedications and slow titration per our allergy desensitization protocol. Also during and after cycle 2, she developed Grade 2 alopecia with complete scalp hair loss, which was anticipated and for which she was fitted through our oncology wig program. She also developed Grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy in the fingertips bilaterally, which has been monitored closely.

Cycle 3 was administered according to the desensitization protocol for carbo and was completed without a repeat hypersensitivity reaction. She returns today at the mid-point of her 6-cycle regimen for assessment.

Currently, Ms. Osei-Bonsu reports the following symptoms:
- Fatigue: She describes persistent fatigue, which she rates as moderate — she is managing her household but has required naps on most days and has reduced her working hours. This is consistent with Grade 2 chemotherapy-related fatigue.
- Peripheral neuropathy: Tingling and numbness in the fingertips bilaterally, and she now notes early involvement of the toes. She can perform all fine motor tasks (writing, buttoning) but notes that fine manipulation is slightly slower. She does not describe pain. This is Grade 1-2 sensory neuropathy.
- Nausea: Mild nausea during the first 3-4 days of each cycle, well-controlled with ondansetron 8 mg TID and prochlorperazine PRN. No vomiting. No diarrhea.
- Alopecia: Complete scalp hair loss, as well as loss of eyebrows and eyelashes. She is using the wig provided and reports that the hair loss, while distressing, is manageable. She is engaged with the oncology support group which has been helpful.
- Appetite: Mildly reduced but she is maintaining adequate caloric intake. No significant weight change since initiating chemotherapy.
- Musculoskeletal: Mild diffuse myalgias and arthralgias for 3-4 days post-infusion (consistent with Taxol-related arthralgia/myalgia syndrome), resolving by day 5-6 of each cycle. She is using acetaminophen 1,000 mg every 6 hours as needed with partial relief.
- No febrile episodes. She checks her temperature at home per instructions and has had no fever. She has not required urgent care or emergency room visits since initiating chemotherapy.
- No shortness of breath, chest pain, or palpitations.
- No unusual bleeding or bruising.
- No mouth sores.
- Bowel function: Normal, no constipation or diarrhea. No blood in stool.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Fatigue as above. No fever, chills, or night sweats. Weight stable.
- HEENT: No oral mucositis. Mild dry eyes (likely related to loss of eyelashes). No vision changes.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or edema.
- Respiratory: No dyspnea, cough, or wheezing.
- Gastrointestinal: Mild nausea, well-controlled. No vomiting, diarrhea, or constipation. No abdominal pain or distension.
- Genitourinary: No urinary symptoms. No vaginal discharge or bleeding (post-BSO status).
- Musculoskeletal: PTX-associated arthralgias/myalgias as above. No joint swelling.
- Neurological: Peripheral sensory neuropathy as above. No weakness, gait difficulties, or hearing changes.
- Skin: Alopecia as above. No rash, erythema, or desquamation. No nail changes noted yet.
- Hematologic: No bleeding, bruising, or petechiae. No lymphadenopathy noted by patient.
- Psychiatric: Patient describes ongoing anxiety about the cancer diagnosis and its implications, but reports that the support group has been very helpful. She continues to have difficult moments emotionally but overall is coping better than at the start of treatment.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital signs: Temperature 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 78 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight: 62.8 kg (stable compared to pre-chemotherapy baseline of 64 kg, approximately 1.2 kg loss — within acceptable range, no concern for significant nutritional decline at this time).

General: Well-appearing woman, appears comfortable and in no acute distress. Alopecia evident (scalp, eyebrows, eyelashes). Wig in place.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Anicteric sclerae. Dry eyes (bilateral). Oral mucosa moist without ulceration, erythema, or thrush. No oral mucositis.

Neck: Supple. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpable. Thyroid non-enlarged.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, rhonchi, or crackles. No dullness to percussion.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No palpable masses or organomegaly. Post-laparotomy midline incision healing well, scar flat and without erythema or drainage. No ascites by physical examination (no fluid wave). Bowel sounds present in all four quadrants.

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No calf tenderness. Peripheral pulses intact bilaterally.

Neurological: Alert and oriented ×4. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Reduced light touch sensation in fingertips bilaterally (consistent with Grade 1-2 sensory neuropathy); toe sensation mildly reduced at distal tips bilaterally. Vibration sense preserved at the ankles. Proprioception intact. Gait normal. Romberg negative.

Skin: Diffuse alopecia as noted. No rash or petechiae. Nail beds without hyperpigmentation or Beau's lines noted at this time.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (TODAY):

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 4.1 K/uL (normal range 4.0-11.0)
- ANC: 2.2 K/uL (adequate; no neutropenia)

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 47-year-old woman with FIGO IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (5382insC), who has achieved a complete biochemical and radiographic response following primary debulking surgery (R0 resection) and 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel chemotherapy. CA-125 is now normalized at 11 U/mL, HE4 is normalized, and her post-treatment CT is entirely clean.

1. COMPLETE RESPONSE CONFIRMED:
She has achieved what we most hope for in this disease: R0 surgical debulking, CA-125 normalization, and no radiographic evidence of disease. Given her germline BRCA1 mutation with somatic LOH, high HRD score (68), and this complete response, she is in the best possible position for PARP inhibitor maintenance.

2. PARP INHIBITOR MAINTENANCE THERAPY:
We discussed at length the strong evidence supporting PARP inhibitor maintenance in this setting. The SOLO-1 trial demonstrated that olaparib (Lynparza) maintenance for 2 years in women with germline BRCA1/2-mutated advanced ovarian cancer who achieved a complete or partial response to first-line platinum-based chemotherapy resulted in a dramatic improvement in progression-free survival — 3-year PFS rate of 60% vs. 27% with placebo, with updated data showing sustained long-term benefit. The PRIMA trial similarly demonstrated benefit for niraparib (Zejula) across the broader HRD population. Given Ms. Osei-Bonsu's germline BRCA1 status and complete response, she has the strongest possible indication for maintenance.

We discussed olaparib (Lynparza 300 mg twice daily) vs. niraparib (Zejula). The patient asked about the dosing schedules; I explained that olaparib is two 150 mg tablets twice daily, whereas niraparib is a once-daily dosing regimen. We discussed potential side effects of each — for niraparib: nausea, fatigue, anemia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia, and hypertension are the main concerns; for olaparib: nausea, fatigue, anemia, and a small but real risk of secondary myeloid malignancy with prolonged use. After discussion, the patient expressed a preference for once-daily dosing for convenience. We will proceed with niraparib (Zejula).

Dosing: Per updated PRIMA individualized dosing guidance and the prescribing information, patients with body weight less than 77 kg may start at niraparib 200 mg once daily to reduce the risk of early hematologic toxicity. Ms. Osei-Bonsu weighs 64 kg; baseline platelets today are 241 K/uL. Her platelets have fully recovered to normal post-chemotherapy. Given weight <77 kg, I will start her at niraparib 200 mg orally once daily taken at bedtime to reduce nausea.

Monitoring plan: CBC weekly for the first 8 weeks, then monthly. Blood pressure monitoring at each visit given reported hypertensive potential. CA-125 every 3 months. Next CT at 12 months from completion of chemotherapy per surveillance protocol.

Plan to continue for a total of 3 years from initiation per SOLO-1 data (though individualized based on tolerability and response).

Patient counseled in detail on:
- Nausea (most common; take at bedtime, use ondansetron 8 mg PRN, prochlorperazine as backup)
- Anemia and thrombocytopenia (dose-limiting; must monitor CBC closely; to present to infusion center or ED for significant bleeding)
- Neutropenia (less common; to call if fever >38°C)
- Hypertension (common; will monitor BP)
- Fatigue (may overlap with post-chemotherapy fatigue; expected to improve over time)
- MDS/AML risk (rare, <1% long-term; benefit clearly outweighs risk at this stage)
- No grapefruit or grapefruit juice (mild CYP interactions)
- Pregnancy: not applicable given surgical menopause from BSO, but menopause discussed; referred to gynecology for menopausal symptom management as needed

SURGICAL PATHOLOGY REPORT

Patient Name: Osei-Bonsu, Margaret
Date of Birth: [on file]
Medical Record Number: [on file]
Accession Number: SP-[on file]
Ordering Physician: Gynecologic Oncology Service
Procedure: Diagnostic Laparoscopy with Omental Biopsy
Clinical Indication: Bilateral adnexal masses, omental caking, ascites; rule out malignancy
Specimen Received: Fresh and formalin-fixed tissue

---

SPECIMEN(S) SUBMITTED:

  A. Omentum, biopsy ("omental cake")
  B. Peritoneal washing fluid, cytology (submitted concurrently; see cytology report addendum)

---

CLINICAL HISTORY:
47-year-old female presenting with a 4-month history of progressive abdominal distension, ascites, bilateral adnexal masses (right 7.2 cm, left 5.8 cm on CT), and omental caking. CA-125 markedly elevated at 3,847 U/mL. HE4: 412 pmol/L. Family history notable for maternal aunt with breast cancer at age 38 and maternal grandmother with ovarian cancer at age 55. Diagnostic laparoscopy performed to obtain tissue for histologic diagnosis and to assess surgical feasibility. Omental biopsy obtained laparoscopically.

---

GROSS DESCRIPTION:

Specimen A: Received fresh and subsequently fixed in 10% neutral buffered formalin is an irregular fragment of fibroadipose tissue consistent with omentum, measuring 14.0 x 9.0 x 3.0 cm and weighing 187 grams. The outer surface is tan-white to gray and bosselated. On serial sectioning, the specimen is largely replaced by confluent gray-white, firm, rubbery tumor deposits with focal areas of chalky-white calcification and central necrosis. The normal lobular adipose architecture of the omentum is almost entirely effaced. No discrete cystic structures identified. Representative sections submitted in cassettes A1 through A7.

Specimen B: Received is 180 mL of red-tinged fluid labeled "peritoneal washing." The specimen is processed by cytocentrifugation and ThinPrep liquid-based cytology with Papanicolaou staining. Cell block prepared from the residual pellet. See cytology addendum.

---

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:

Specimen A — Omentum, biopsy:
Sections show fibroadipose tissue of the omentum extensively infiltrated and largely replaced by malignant epithelial neoplasm. The tumor is arranged in sheets, irregular papillary clusters, slit-like glandular spaces, and solid nests. The neoplastic cells are large and polygonal with moderate to abundant pale eosinophilic cytoplasm. The nuclei are markedly enlarged, vesicular, and demonstrate severe pleomorphism with prominent, often cherry-red macronucleoli. Nuclear contours are irregular. Mitotic figures are numerous and include atypical forms (3-5 atypical mitoses per high-power field in the most active areas). Apoptotic bodies are conspicuous.

Psammoma bodies (laminated calcific concretions) are identified within the tumor nests and in areas of necrosis, a feature highly characteristic of serous carcinoma of Mullerian origin. Focal areas of geographic necrosis are present, consistent with high-grade biology. The tumor is associated with a desmoplastic stromal reaction composed of dense fibrous tissue with intermixed inflammatory infiltrate including scattered lymphocytes and plasma cells. Lymphovascular invasion is identified in at least one focus.

No mucinous differentiation is identified. No intracellular mucin by routine staining. No signet ring cells. No squamous differentiation. No clear cell morphology. No endometrioid glandular architecture. No evidence of low-grade serous carcinoma morphology (no rounded papillae with fibrovascular cores, no low nuclear grade). The overall morphologic appearance is that of a high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC), FIGO grade 3.

---

IMMUNOHISTOCHEMICAL STUDIES:

A directed immunohistochemical panel was applied to formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded tissue sections from Specimen A. Results are as follows:

  WT-1 (Wilms Tumor-1):       POSITIVE — diffuse strong nuclear staining (3+) in >90% of tumor cells
  PAX8:                        POSITIVE — diffuse nuclear staining (3+) in >90% of tumor cells
  p53:                         ABERRANT — diffuse strong overexpression pattern ("block-type" positivity, 3+) in >95% of tumor cells; consistent with a TP53 gain-of-function missense mutation
  CK7 (cytokeratin 7):        POSITIVE — diffuse cytoplasmic staining (3+)
  CK20 (cytokeratin 20):      NEGATIVE — no staining in tumor cells
  CA-125 (MUC16):             POSITIVE — diffuse membranous/cytoplasmic staining (2-3+)
  ER (estrogen receptor):     WEAKLY POSITIVE — nuclear staining in approximately 30% of tumor cells (1+)
  PR (progesterone receptor): NEGATIVE
  Napsin-A:                   NEGATIVE — no staining
  CDX2:                       NEGATIVE — no staining
  SATB2:                      NEGATIVE — no staining
  HER2/neu (4B5):             2+ (equivocal; FISH for HER2 amplification: PENDING — see addendum)

  Ki-67 (MIB-1) proliferation index: Approximately 78% of tumor nuclei positive, confirming high-grade biology.

Interpretation of IHC panel: The combined expression of WT-1, PAX8, CK7, and CA-125, with negative CK20, negative napsin-A, and absent PR, along with aberrant p53 pattern, is consistent with high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) of Mullerian origin. Primary ovarian/fallopian tube/peritoneal origin is favored in the clinical context. Primary gastrointestinal, lung adenocarcinoma, and urothelial primaries are effectively excluded by this panel. Weakly positive ER (30%) is seen in a subset of HGSC and is not inconsistent with this diagnosis.

---

ANCILLARY STUDIES:

  - HER2 FISH (fluorescence in situ hybridization): PENDING — specimen submitted to reference laboratory; results to be reported as addendum upon receipt
  - Germline genetic testing: Not performed on this specimen; patient referred for clinical germline testing through genetic counseling service
  - Molecular / NGS testing: Tissue banked for potential future molecular profiling per institutional protocol; not performed at this time

---

CYTOLOGY ADDENDUM — Specimen B (Peritoneal Washing):

The peritoneal washing cytology preparation demonstrates numerous clusters and three-dimensional papillary aggregates of atypical epithelial cells with features of malignancy. Individual malignant cells display enlarged, pleomorphic nuclei with irregular nuclear membranes, coarse chromatin, and prominent nucleoli. The cytoplasm is moderate and mildly vacuolated. Background shows blood, fibrin strands, scattered mesothelial cells, and inflammatory cells. Psammoma bodies are identified in multiple fields. The cell block sections confirm the presence of malignant epithelial cells.

Conclusion: POSITIVE FOR MALIGNANT CELLS. Morphologic features are consistent with high-grade serous carcinoma.

---

DIAGNOSIS:

A. OMENTUM, BIOPSY ("omental cake"):
   METASTATIC HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA
   — Diffuse omental involvement with geographic necrosis, psammoma bodies, and desmoplastic stroma
   — Lymphovascular invasion identified
   — Immunohistochemical profile: WT-1(+), PAX8(+), CK7(+), CA-125(+), p53 aberrant (diffuse strong overexpression, gain-of-function pattern), ER weakly positive (30%), CK20(-), napsin-A(-)
   — Ki-67 proliferation index: ~78%
   — Morphologic and immunohistochemical profile consistent with primary Mullerian (ovarian/fallopian tube) origin
   — HER2 FISH: PENDING (addendum to follow)

B. PERITONEAL WASHING, CYTOLOGY:
   POSITIVE FOR MALIGNANT CELLS
   — High-grade serous carcinoma, consistent with primary Mullerian origin

---

SYNOPTIC SUMMARY (CAP Protocol for Carcinoma of Unknown Primary, Peritoneum/Omentum):

  Procedure:                    Laparoscopic biopsy
  Specimen site:                Omentum
  Tumor configuration:          Diffuse infiltration (omental cake)
  Histologic type:              High-grade serous carcinoma
  Histologic grade:             FIGO Grade 3 (high grade)
  Mitotic rate:                 High (>10 mitoses/10 HPF)
  Necrosis:                     Present (geographic)
  Psammoma bodies:              Present
  Lymphovascular invasion:      Present
  Peritoneal washing cytology:  Positive for malignant cells
  Additional pathologic findings: Desmoplastic stroma; marked nuclear pleomorphism
  Ancillary studies:            See above

---

PATHOLOGIST'S NOTE:

This high-grade serous carcinoma involving the omentum, in a 47-year-old woman with bilateral adnexal masses, marked CA-125 elevation (3,847 U/mL), elevated HE4, positive peritoneal cytology, and a family history of breast and ovarian cancer, is strongly consistent with an advanced-stage primary ovarian or fallopian tube high-grade serous carcinoma with omental metastasis. The aberrant p53 immunostaining pattern (diffuse strong "block-type" overexpression in >95% of cells) is consistent with a TP53 gain-of-function mutation, as expected in the vast majority of HGSC. Correlation with clinical, radiologic, and operative findings is recommended for definitive primary site classification. Germline BRCA1/2 testing is strongly recommended given the clinical and family history, and has been initiated per the ordering clinician.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu was initially diagnosed with FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous carcinoma at age 47, presenting with a four-month history of progressive abdominal distension, pelvic pressure, early satiety, and a 6 kg unintentional weight loss. Workup demonstrated bilateral ovarian masses (right 7.2 cm, left 5.8 cm), omental caking, peritoneal carcinomatosis with implants up to 2.3 cm, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenopathy, and a markedly elevated CA-125 of 3,847 U/mL. Germline testing confirmed a pathogenic BRCA1 variant (c.5266dupC, p.Gln1756ProfsTer74, the 5382insC founder mutation), and somatic tumor sequencing (Foundation One CDx) demonstrated loss of heterozygosity at the BRCA1 locus with a high HRD score of 68 and high genomic LOH of 31%, confirming homologous recombination deficiency.

She underwent primary debulking surgery with total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, resection of peritoneal implants, and right hemidiaphragmatic stripping, achieving complete gross resection (R0). Pathologic staging confirmed pT3c pN1 disease with 5 of 27 lymph nodes involved and no residual macroscopic disease at the operative margins. She then completed six cycles of adjuvant carboplatin (AUC 5) and paclitaxel (175 mg/m2, dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2 for cycles 5 and 6 due to cumulative Grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy). CLINICAL INDICATION:

TECHNIQUE:
Multiphase CT urogram of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed on a 64-slice multidetector CT scanner following administration of 100 mL Omnipaque 350 (iohexol) intravenous contrast. Three acquisition phases were obtained: corticomedullary phase (40 seconds), nephrographic phase (90 seconds), and delayed urographic phase (10 minutes post-injection) to opacify the collecting systems and ureters. Coronal and sagittal reformats were generated and reviewed. Multiplanar reconstructions in the coronal plane were used for assessment of the ureters in their entirety. No oral contrast administered. Radiation dose: CTDIvol 12.4 mGy; DLP 524 mGy-cm.

Prior CT of the abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast performed approximately 6 months previously (surveillance scan at 24 months from completion of chemotherapy), which demonstrated no evidence of disease recurrence.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 49-year-old woman with a history of germline BRCA1-associated HGSC of the ovaries and fallopian tubes, now presenting for her scheduled oncology visit at the 36-month mark from her initial diagnosis. She is here to discuss completion of PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy and transition to routine surveillance. She has no new complaints.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu was diagnosed with high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC, approximately 3 years ago. She initially presented with a 4-month history of progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, pelvic pressure, urinary frequency, and a 6 kg unintentional weight loss. Workup revealed bilateral ovarian masses (right 7.2 cm, left 5.8 cm), omental caking, peritoneal carcinomatosis (PCI approximately 18 on preoperative imaging), bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenopathy, and markedly elevated CA-125 at 3,847 U/mL. Germline testing confirmed a pathogenic BRCA1 variant (5382insC founder mutation). She subsequently underwent primary debulking surgery with exploratory laparotomy, TAH-BSO, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, resection of peritoneal implants, and diaphragmatic stripping, achieving an R0 (no gross residual disease) resection. Surgical pathology confirmed stage pT3c pN1, with STIC of the fimbriated end of the right fallopian tube, four pelvic lymph nodes and one para-aortic lymph node positive, and multiple peritoneal implants cleared. She then received six cycles of adjuvant carboplatin (AUC 5) and paclitaxel (175 mg/m2, dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2 for cycles 5-6 due to cumulative peripheral neuropathy). Her course was complicated by a Grade 2 carboplatin hypersensitivity reaction during cycle 2, managed with desensitization protocol, and by Grade 2 anemia requiring transfusion after cycle 4. She completed all six cycles and achieved a complete biochemical and radiographic response, with CA-125 normalizing to 11 U/mL post-chemotherapy and CT imaging showing no evidence of disease.

Given her germline BRCA1 status and complete response to platinum-based chemotherapy, she was initiated on niraparib (Zejula) maintenance therapy at 200 mg orally once daily, consistent with the SOLO-1 trial evidence base and FDA-approved indication. Approximately 3 weeks after starting niraparib, she developed Grade 3 thrombocytopenia (nadir platelets 58,000/uL), necessitating a treatment hold and subsequent dose reduction to 100 mg daily upon platelet recovery. At the reduced dose of 100 mg, she has experienced chronic low-grade thrombocytopenia — platelets have generally hovered in the 98,000–128,000/uL range (Grade 1-2) — which has been managed with close monitoring and without further dose reduction or interruption. She also developed niraparib-associated hypertension, managed with amlodipine, which has been successfully tapered now that she is approaching cessation of niraparib. She has otherwise tolerated niraparib 100 mg well.

Over the course of her maintenance therapy, serial CA-125 measurements have remained consistently within the normal range (range 7–11 U/mL). Surveillance CT scans at 12 months and 24 months post-chemotherapy completion demonstrated sustained no evidence of disease. She had an unrelated intercurrent issue of a 4 mm non-obstructing right renal calculus at approximately 30 months from diagnosis, which passed spontaneously on conservative management.

She has now completed approximately 28 months of niraparib maintenance therapy at the 100 mg dose. The original intent was to continue for 36 months per the SOLO-1 trial protocol, which demonstrated a sustained progression-free survival benefit in germline BRCA-mutated patients who achieved complete response. However, today's visit involves a shared decision-making discussion regarding the timing of maintenance therapy cessation. Per the updated PRIMA and SOLO-1 long-term data, and in the context of her excellent sustained remission, tolerability profile, and patient preference, as well as the cumulative hematologic and quality-of-life burden of long-term PARP inhibition, she and I have engaged in a detailed discussion about whether to continue to the full 36 months or to conclude therapy at this time point. After thorough counseling, the decision has been made to discontinue niraparib at 28 months of maintenance (approximately 36 months from diagnosis), representing a clinically reasonable course given the totality of evidence.

Today she has no new symptoms and feels well overall.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 49-year-old woman with germline BRCA1-associated (5382insC) high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC, who achieved an R0 resection at primary debulking surgery and a complete biochemical and radiographic response to six cycles of adjuvant carboplatin/paclitaxel. She has now been on niraparib maintenance therapy for approximately 28 months (at the dose-reduced level of 100 mg daily following early Grade 3 thrombocytopenia). She continues to be in sustained complete remission with CA-125 consistently at 9 U/mL, normal biochemical parameters, and excellent performance status.

1. PARP INHIBITOR MAINTENANCE THERAPY — DISCONTINUATION:
We had a lengthy and substantive shared decision-making discussion today regarding the timing of niraparib cessation. Per the SOLO-1 trial (Moore et al., NEJM 2018 and updated long-term analysis), olaparib maintenance for 2 years in germline BRCA-mutated patients with complete or partial response to first-line platinum demonstrated a landmark improvement in progression-free survival, with benefit extending well beyond the treatment period. The PRIMA trial provides supporting evidence for niraparib. The originally planned duration was 3 years (36 months from initiation). She has completed 28 months of niraparib, and she has been on PARP inhibition as a class for this duration.

We discussed the following in our shared decision-making conversation:
- The evidence base supporting 2–3 years of PARP inhibitor maintenance in this setting.
- Her cumulative toxicity burden: chronic Grade 1-2 thrombocytopenia throughout maintenance, hypertension requiring pharmacologic management (now well-controlled), and the psychosocial burden of ongoing medication and monitoring.
- Her sustained and durable complete remission — 28+ months of surveillance with no biochemical or radiographic evidence of recurrence.
- The fact that most of the progression-free survival benefit of PARP inhibitor maintenance appears to accrue within the first 24 months of maintenance, with diminishing marginal benefit thereafter in the maintained-remission population.
- Patient preference: Ms. Osei-Bonsu has thoughtfully considered this decision and expresses a clear preference to discontinue niraparib at this juncture, accepting the theoretical residual risk of recurrence in exchange for freedom from ongoing hematologic monitoring, antihypertensive medication, and the daily medication burden.
- The option to restart PARP inhibitor or pursue other therapy at the time of recurrence, should recurrence ever occur, was discussed.

Decision: Niraparib (Zejula) is discontinued effective today. She was counseled that platelets are expected to recover over the coming weeks to months following cessation, as we have observed with prior holds. She was advised to watch for any symptoms that might suggest thrombocytopenia (unusual bruising, petechiae, prolonged bleeding) in the interim before platelet recovery, though at her current platelet level this is low-risk.

2. HYPERTENSION — AMLODIPINE TAPER:
Blood pressure today is 122/76 mmHg, well-controlled. Given niraparib cessation, the contribution of PARP inhibitor to her hypertension will resolve. Plan to taper amlodipine from 5 mg to 2.5 mg in 4 weeks, then reassess for discontinuation. Coordinating with her primary care physician. She is instructed to monitor blood pressure at home biweekly.

3. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY:
Residual Grade 0-1 peripheral sensory neuropathy in bilateral toes, largely resolved from its peak during and after chemotherapy. This is stable and non-limiting. Duloxetine 30 mg daily will be continued for now; reassess at next visit with consideration for taper given the marked improvement.

4. SURVEILLANCE PLAN (post-maintenance):
Now that niraparib is discontinued, she will transition into an active surveillance protocol consistent with current NCCN and SGO guidelines for HGSC in sustained remission after PARP inhibitor maintenance:
- CA-125 every 3 months for the next 2 years, then every 6 months thereafter.
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis every 6 months for the next 2 years, then annually thereafter (or sooner if symptoms or rising CA-125).
- Surveillance visits: every 3 months for the next 1 year, then every 6 months.
- Patient educated on symptoms of recurrence: new or worsening abdominal pain or bloating, increased abdominal girth, early satiety, change in bowel or bladder habits, unintentional weight loss. She is instructed to contact our office promptly for any new or concerning symptoms rather than waiting for a scheduled appointment.
- Next appointment: 3 months (with CA-125) and a CT scan around that timeframe.

5. BREAST CANCER SURVEILLANCE (germline BRCA1):
Given her confirmed germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant, she remains at significantly elevated lifetime risk for breast cancer. Current breast surveillance:
- Annual bilateral breast MRI alternating with mammography every 6 months per NCCN guidelines for BRCA1 carriers.
- Most recent bilateral breast MRI (outside institution): no suspicious findings on file.
- Risk-reducing bilateral mastectomy (RRM) was discussed again today. She declines RRM at this time but understands the risk-reduction benefit and remains open to revisiting the discussion. She is to continue intensive surveillance.
- Referral for follow-up with hereditary cancer genetics program to ensure she is enrolled in the appropriate breast surveillance program and to reassess risk-reduction options annually.

6. PSYCHOSOCIAL AND QUALITY OF LIFE:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu has demonstrated remarkable resilience throughout this journey. She has returned to full-time teaching, is engaged in yoga and hiking, and is actively involved in the oncology support group. She describes her overall wellbeing as excellent. We discussed the potential psychological impact of transitioning off active treatment — the so-called "Damocles syndrome" — and the anxiety that some patients experience when they no longer feel "protected" by ongoing therapy. She acknowledges some background health anxiety but feels well-equipped to manage it. Referral to the survivorship clinic was offered; she is interested and will be scheduled accordingly.

7. FAMILY GENETIC IMPLICATIONS:
Patient's daughter (age 22) has tested positive for BRCA1 and is engaged with genetic counseling and risk-reduction planning — appropriate. Patient's sister (age 44) has been referred; encouraged to follow up on the status of her sister's evaluation at next visit. Test Methodology:

GERMLINE RESULT: POSITIVE — PATHOGENIC VARIANT IDENTIFIED

Gene: BRCA1
Nucleotide Change: c.5266dupC
Protein Change: p.Gln1756ProfsTer74
Alternate Designation: 5382insC
Zygosity: Heterozygous
Clinical Classification: PATHOGENIC
ClinVar Accession: ClinVar ID 37523
Inheritance: Autosomal dominant

Interpretation:
The c.5266dupC (5382insC) variant in BRCA1 is one of the most well-characterized pathogenic variants in the BRCA1 gene. This single-nucleotide insertion creates a frameshift at codon 1756, introducing a premature stop codon 74 amino acids downstream (p.Gln1756ProfsTer74). This results in a truncated, non-functional BRCA1 protein with loss of the BRCT domain, which is essential for DNA damage response and homologous recombination repair. The variant has been documented as a founder mutation in populations of Eastern European Ashkenazi Jewish ancestry and has been independently documented in West African populations, including those of Ghanaian descent, consistent with this patient's background. This variant has been observed in thousands of individuals with breast and ovarian cancer and segregates with disease in multiple high-risk families. Functional studies demonstrate absent or markedly reduced BRCA1 protein activity. Classification: Pathogenic (Class 5) per ACMG/AMP criteria (PVS1, PS1, PS4, PP1).

Clinical Significance:
Germline pathogenic BRCA1 variants confer a markedly elevated lifetime risk of epithelial ovarian cancer (approximately 40–46% by age 70) and breast cancer (approximately 55–72% by age 70). They are also associated with modestly elevated risk of pancreatic cancer. This variant is directly relevant to the patient's diagnosis of high-grade serous carcinoma and has important implications for systemic treatment selection (specifically, PARP inhibitor eligibility) and for cascade testing of at-risk family members.

Secondary/Additional Findings:
- BRCA2: No pathogenic or likely pathogenic variant identified. No large rearrangements detected.
- Lynch Syndrome Genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2): No pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants identified. MSI status by tumor testing reported separately.
- PALB2, ATM, RAD51C, RAD51D, BRIP1, CHEK2: No pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants identified. One variant of uncertain significance (VUS) identified in ATM: c.7271T>G (p.Val2424Gly), clinical significance unknown; not considered actionable at this time.
- EPCAM: No large deletion involving EPCAM detected.

Family History Correlation:
Family history is notable for a maternal aunt with breast cancer at age 38 and maternal grandmother with ovarian cancer at age 55, a pattern highly consistent with autosomal dominant BRCA1 hereditary breast and ovarian cancer (HBOC) syndrome. Cascade genetic testing is strongly recommended for first-degree relatives including siblings and adult children.

SOMATIC ALTERATIONS DETECTED

1. TP53 p.R248W — SHORT VARIANT (MISSENSE MUTATION)
   VAF: 68%
   Classification: Pathogenic — Gain-of-Function
   The R248W hotspot missense mutation is among the most common TP53 alterations in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, occurring in >96% of HGSC tumors. R248W results in loss of wild-type tumor suppressor function and gain of oncogenic function, including promotion of cell migration, invasion, and resistance to apoptosis. The high VAF (68%) in the context of estimated 65% tumor cellularity indicates clonal, homozygous or near-homozygous TP53 alteration, consistent with the diffuse aberrant p53 overexpression seen on immunohistochemistry (p53 diffuse strong, consistent with gain-of-function pattern). This is a ubiquitous driver alteration in HGSC and consistent with the histologic diagnosis.

2. BRCA1 p.Gln1756ProfsTer74 (c.5266dupC) — SHORT VARIANT (FRAMESHIFT)
   VAF: 82%
   Classification: Pathogenic
   This is the same frameshift variant identified in the germline at heterozygous state. The somatic VAF of 82% significantly exceeds the expected ~50% for heterozygous germline retention, providing strong evidence for loss of heterozygosity (LOH) at the BRCA1 locus in the tumor. LOH at BRCA1 with retention of the pathogenic allele is the canonical mechanism of biallelic BRCA1 inactivation in HGSC and is required for full homologous recombination deficiency (HRD). This finding confirms complete somatic loss of BRCA1 function in this tumor and is the principal molecular driver of the high HRD score (see below).

3. RB1 — DEEP DELETION (HOMOZYGOUS LOSS)
   Copy Number: <0.5 (estimated homozygous deletion)
   Classification: Likely Pathogenic
   Homozygous deletion of RB1 represents complete biallelic loss of the retinoblastoma tumor suppressor, resulting in unchecked cell cycle progression through the G1/S checkpoint. RB1 loss is identified in approximately 20–30% of high-grade serous ovarian carcinomas and is associated with a more aggressive phenotype. RB1 deletion may also have implications for sensitivity to certain CDK4/6 inhibitor-based strategies, though this is investigational in ovarian cancer.

4. CCNE1 — AMPLIFICATION
   Copy Number: 6.2 (high-level amplification)
   Classification: Likely Pathogenic
   Cyclin E1 (CCNE1) amplification results in overexpression of cyclin E1 protein, leading to aberrant CDK2-mediated phosphorylation of RB1 and dysregulated S-phase entry. CCNE1 amplification is observed in approximately 20% of HGSC and is typically mutually exclusive with BRCA1/2 biallelic inactivation, as it represents an alternative mechanism of HRD and genomic instability. However, co-occurrence with BRCA1 LOH, as seen in this case, has been described and may indicate evolutionary selection for additional drivers of genomic instability. CCNE1 amplification has been associated with relative platinum resistance in some studies; its coexistence with high HRD score in this patient warrants close monitoring of platinum response.

5. NF1 p.R1362* — SHORT VARIANT (NONSENSE)
   VAF: 18%
   Classification: Likely Pathogenic (Subclonal)
   This nonsense mutation in NF1 (neurofibromin 1), a RAS-GAP tumor suppressor, is present at a subclonal VAF of 18%, suggesting it arose in a minority tumor subpopulation following the founding TP53 and BRCA1 alterations. NF1 loss results in constitutive RAS-MAPK pathway activation. Subclonal NF1 mutations are recognized in HGSC as secondary events and may contribute to intratumoral heterogeneity and adaptive resistance. Clinical significance in this setting is uncertain; no approved targeted therapies directly targeting NF1 loss are available for ovarian cancer.

6. PIK3CA p.E545K — SHORT VARIANT (MISSENSE)
   VAF: 12%
   Classification: Pathogenic (Subclonal)
   The E545K hotspot mutation in PIK3CA (PI3-kinase catalytic subunit alpha) is a well-characterized activating mutation in the helical domain, resulting in constitutive PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway activation. Present at a subclonal VAF of 12%, this appears to represent a minority clonal event. While PIK3CA mutations are actionable in certain cancer types (e.g., PIK3CA-mutant breast cancer with alpelisib), their clinical significance as subclonal events in HGSC is investigational. PI3K pathway activation may contribute to treatment resistance in a subpopulation of tumor cells.

7. NOTCH3 p.R1626C — SHORT VARIANT (MISSENSE)
   VAF: 9%
   Classification: Variant of Uncertain Significance (VUS)
   This missense variant in NOTCH3 is present at a very low VAF (9%), consistent with a late, minor subclonal event. NOTCH3 alterations have been described in various cancers; however, the functional consequence of p.R1626C has not been definitively established. This variant is classified as a VUS and is not considered actionable at this time.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 47-year-old woman of West African (Ghanaian) descent who presents to the gynecologic oncology clinic for initial consultation following a new diagnosis of high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC, with peritoneal carcinomatosis. She is referred by her gynecologist after diagnostic laparoscopy and omental biopsy confirmed the diagnosis. She presents today with her husband to discuss surgical planning and overall management.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu reports a 4-month history of progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, and a 6 kg (approximately 13 lb) unintentional weight loss. She initially attributed the bloating and fullness to dietary changes and work-related stress, and delayed seeking medical attention for several weeks. Over the past 6-8 weeks, she has noticed increasing bilateral lower pelvic pressure and a sensation of heaviness in the lower abdomen. She also reports urinary frequency without dysuria, urgency, or hematuria, which she attributes to external pressure from a pelvic mass. On further questioning, she describes fatigue that has progressively worsened over the same time course, now limiting her ability to sustain a full workday without rest periods. She denies nausea, vomiting, melena, hematochezia, or any changes in bowel habits. She denies hemoptysis, cough, or chest pain.

Her symptom onset prompted referral from her primary care physician to her gynecologist after a pelvic ultrasound demonstrated bilateral complex adnexal masses. Subsequent CA-125 was markedly elevated at 3,847 U/mL and HE4 was elevated at 412 pmol/L, both strongly suggestive of epithelial ovarian malignancy. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis demonstrated bilateral complex adnexal masses (right 7.2 x 6.1 x 5.8 cm, left 5.8 x 4.3 cm), moderate-volume ascites, omental caking with confluent soft tissue density measuring up to 11 cm in maximum dimension, multiple peritoneal implants along the right paracolic gutter, left pelvic peritoneum, and small bowel mesentery (largest 2.3 cm), and bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenopathy (right external iliac 1.6 cm, left para-aortic 1.8 cm). No pleural effusion, pulmonary nodules, or parenchymal liver metastases were identified. The CT impression noted disease appearing surgically resectable without suprarenal or supradiaphragmatic involvement, with an estimated Peritoneal Cancer Index of approximately 18.

Diagnostic laparoscopy and omental biopsy were subsequently performed. Pathology from the omental biopsy confirmed high-grade serous carcinoma of Mullerian origin: sheets and papillary clusters of highly atypical epithelial cells with prominent nucleoli, marked nuclear pleomorphism, and abundant mitoses including atypical forms; psammoma bodies present; immunohistochemical profile showing WT-1 positive (diffuse), PAX8 positive (diffuse), p53 aberrant (diffuse strong overexpression consistent with TP53 gain-of-function mutation), ER weakly positive, CK7 positive, CK20 negative, and CA-125 positive; Ki-67 proliferation index 78%. Peritoneal washings were positive for malignant cells with serous features.

Germline genetic testing has also been completed and results have been reviewed with the patient: she was found to carry a pathogenic germline BRCA1 variant c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756ProfsTer74, the 5382insC founder mutation), classified as pathogenic. Tumor NGS (Foundation One CDx on the omental biopsy specimen) demonstrated somatic TP53 p.R248W (VAF 68%), BRCA1 LOH at the germline locus (VAF 82%, consistent with loss of heterozygosity), RB1 deep deletion, CCNE1 amplification, and subclonal NF1 and PIK3CA alterations. HRD score was 68 (positive) with genomic LOH of 31%. Tumor mutational burden was low (4.2 mut/Mb) and microsatellite status was stable. The germline BRCA1 variant with somatic LOH is highly predictive of PARP inhibitor sensitivity, which has important implications for maintenance therapy planning following chemotherapy.

The patient has been informed of her diagnosis and understands its gravity. She is emotionally distressed, particularly regarding her prognosis and the implications of the BRCA1 finding for her daughter and sister. She and her husband have many questions today. She was referred to oncology social work prior to this visit.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 47-year-old premenopausal woman with newly diagnosed high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) of bilateral ovaries and fallopian tubes, FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0), with extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis, ascites, and bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenopathy. Her tumor carries the hallmarks of HGSC: WT-1/PAX8 positivity, p53 aberrant pattern, and an extremely elevated CA-125. She carries a germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (5382insC), and her tumor demonstrates somatic LOH at the BRCA1 locus with a high HRD score — a highly favorable predictive biomarker for both platinum sensitivity and PARP inhibitor sensitivity.

1. SURGICAL PLANNING — PRIMARY DEBULKING SURGERY (PDS):
Based on the CT imaging, diagnostic laparoscopy findings, and her overall excellent functional status (ECOG 1), Ms. Osei-Bonsu is deemed a good candidate for primary debulking surgery with the goal of achieving complete gross resection (R0). The importance of achieving R0 resection — the single most important prognostic surgical variable in advanced HGSC — was discussed in detail. Planned surgical procedure includes exploratory laparotomy, total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (TAH-BSO), infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy (given the proximity of the omental and peritoneal disease), bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, and peritoneal implant resection. The possibility of upper abdominal surgery including diaphragmatic stripping was discussed. Based on current disease distribution, bowel resection does not appear necessary, though this cannot be fully excluded until exploration. The surgical complexity score is estimated as moderate-high, and the case will be approached with full colorectal surgery backup.

Risks discussed at length with patient and husband: bleeding, infection, wound complications, injury to bowel, bladder, and ureters, lymphocele formation, prolonged ileus, deep venous thrombosis and pulmonary embolism (VTE prophylaxis will be employed perioperatively and for 28 days postoperatively), need for ostomy (low but non-zero probability), ICU admission, and risks related to anesthesia. Expected hospital stay: 4-6 days. Patient verbalized understanding of risks and benefits.

2. ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY PLANNING:
Following surgery (anticipated 4-6 weeks for recovery), the patient will proceed with platinum-based adjuvant chemotherapy: carbo (AUC 5-6) plus Taxol (175 mg/m2) IV every 21 days for 6 cycles. This is the international standard of care for FIGO stage III HGSC following optimal debulking surgery, supported by GOG-0158 and ICON3 data, and consistent with current NCCN guidelines. The role of Avastin (Avastin) in the upfront setting was discussed (ICON7, GOG-0218 data); given the planned R0 resection (which confers a more favorable prognosis), the incremental benefit of bev was felt to be modest and bev was not planned to be added to the upfront regimen. Intraperitoneal chemotherapy (IP/IV) was also discussed; given contemporary data and patient preference for IV access, standard IV dosing was favored.

3. MAINTENANCE THERAPY PLANNING:
Given her germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant with somatic LOH and the expectation that she will achieve a complete or near-complete response to platinum-based chemotherapy (given her HRD-high tumor biology), she is an excellent candidate for PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy following chemotherapy. Options include Lynparza (Lynparza) and Zejula (Zejula). Preliminary discussions were initiated; final decision will be deferred to medical oncology consultation and post-chemotherapy response assessment. The SOLO-1 trial demonstrated a significant improvement in progression-free survival with ola maintenance in germline BRCA1/2 patients achieving response to first-line platinum therapy; PRIMA also demonstrated benefit of nira in HRD-positive patients. Total planned maintenance duration is 3 years per current evidence.

4. GERMLINE BRCA1 IMPLICATIONS:
The significance of the germline BRCA1 c.5266dupC pathogenic variant was reviewed with the patient. She has an increased lifetime risk of breast cancer (approximately 65-72% by age 80) and of a second primary ovarian/fallopian tube malignancy (mitigated by current BSO). She was counseled that her first-degree relatives (daughter, son, sister) may be at 50% risk of carrying the same variant. Her daughter (age 22) and sister (age 44) were specifically discussed as candidates for genetic counseling and testing. Referral to our hereditary cancer program was placed. Risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy has already been addressed by the current surgery; breast cancer surveillance and risk reduction will need to be coordinated with breast surgery and/or medical genetics following recovery.

5. PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu is visibly distressed and tearful during this consultation, expressing fear about her diagnosis, anxiety regarding her children (particularly the implications of her BRCA1 status for her daughter), and concern about surgical recovery and chemotherapy side effects. She was reassured that the multidisciplinary team will support her throughout. Referral to oncology social work was placed. She was also provided information on the gynecologic oncology patient support group that meets biweekly. Her husband was present and engaged; he was included in all discussions. The importance of open communication with her support network was emphasized.

6. ADDITIONAL CONSIDERATIONS:
- Pre-operative anesthesia and cardiopulmonary evaluation to be completed prior to scheduling.
- Pre-operative bowel preparation will be discussed with the surgical team.
- The patient's mild anemia (Hgb 11.1 g/dL) was noted; iron supplementation already initiated. Type and screen sent. Perioperative transfusion threshold and potential for cell salvage discussed.
- VTE risk: elevated given malignancy, planned major abdominal surgery. Mechanical compression devices intraoperatively; enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneous daily to begin 12-24 hours postoperatively and to continue for 28 days post-discharge per GOG/ASCO VTE guidelines.
- Nutrition consult placed given mild hypoalbuminemia (3.4 g/dL) and 6 kg weight loss; prehabilitation nutritional optimization to be pursued in the brief preoperative window.
- Patient wig referral not yet placed at this visit; to be addressed at subsequent oncology visit.
- Fertility: the patient has completed childbearing (G2P2); bilateral oophorectomy required as part of the operation; premature surgical menopause will result and hormone replacement therapy was briefly discussed, though typically deferred in the immediate postoperative/chemotherapy period. Menopause management to be addressed in follow-up.

The case will be presented at the multidisciplinary gynecologic oncology tumor board for review prior to surgical scheduling. The patient and her husband expressed understanding of the overall plan and agreement to proceed. They were given written materials and the oncology nurse's contact information. Follow-up appointment scheduled in one week to confirm surgical date and complete pre-operative workup.

Time spent in counseling and coordination of care: approximately 75 minutes.
